name: Spec
comment:
Functions for writing specs.

A spec is a collection of scenarios, each of which provides an example that
illustrates a behavior belonging to your program. Each scenario follows
the same basic plan:

1. Configure the initial state of the world (and your program).
2. Perform a sequence of steps.
3. Check your xpectations about the new state of the world after the steps have been performed.

Here's a sample spec for a browser program called `App`:

    Spec.describe "some part of my system"
    [ Spec.scenario "the awesome path" (
        Spec.given (
          Spec.Setup.init (App.init testFlags)
            |> Spec.Setup.withView App.view
            |> Spec.Setup.withUpdate App.update
        )
        |> Spec.when "something happens"
          [ Spec.Markup.target << by [ id "some-button" ]
          , Spec.Markup.Event.click
          ]
        |> Spec.it "does the right thing" (
          Spec.Markup.observeElement
            |> Spec.Markup.query << by [ id "some-words" ]
            |> Spec.expect (Spec.Markup.hasText "something awesome")
        )
      )
    ]

# Creating a Spec
@docs Spec, Scenario, describe, scenario

# Creating the Scenario Script
@docs Script, given, when

# Turn a Script into a Plan
@docs Plan, it, observeThat, expect

# Tag a Scenario
@docs tagged

unions.name: Plan
unions.comment: Represents the full plan (setup, steps, expectations) involved in a scenario.

unions.name: Scenario
unions.comment: Represents a particular scenario in a spec.

unions.name: Script
unions.comment: Represents the setup and steps involved in a scenario.

values.name: describe
values.comment: Specify a description and a list of scenarios that compose the spec.
values.type: String.String -> List.List (Spec.Scenario model msg) -> Spec.Spec model msg

values.name: expect
values.comment: Provide an observer with a claim to evaluate.
values.type: Spec.Claim.Claim a -> Spec.Observer.Observer model a -> Spec.Observer.Expectation model

values.name: given
values.comment:
Provide the `Setup` that represents the state of the world at the start of the scenario.

See `Spec.Setup` for functions to construct this representation.
values.type: Spec.Setup.Setup model msg -> Spec.Script model msg

values.name: it
values.comment: Specify an expectation to be checked once the scenario's steps have been performed.
values.type: String.String -> Spec.Observer.Expectation model -> Spec.Script model msg -> Spec.Plan model msg

values.name: observeThat
values.comment: Specify multiple expectations to be checked once the scenario's steps have been performed.
values.type: List.List (Spec.Script model msg -> Spec.Plan model msg) -> Spec.Script model msg -> Spec.Plan model msg

values.name: scenario
values.comment: Create a scenario with a description and a plan.
values.type: String.String -> Spec.Plan model msg -> Spec.Scenario model msg

values.name: tagged
values.comment:
Associate one or more tags with a scenario.

When you run the spec suite with specific tags specified, only the scenarios
tagged with those tags will be executed.
values.type: List.List String.String -> Spec.Scenario model msg -> Spec.Scenario model msg

values.name: when
values.comment:
Specify a description and the steps involved in a scenario.

Each step is a function from `Step.Context` to `Step.Command`, but usually
you will use steps that are provided by other modules, like `Spec.Markup.Event`.

You may provide multiple `when` blocks as part of a scenario.
values.type: String.String -> List.List (Spec.Step.Context model -> Spec.Step.Command msg) -> Spec.Script model msg -> Spec.Script model msg

name: Spec.Claim

unions.name: Verdict
unions.comment:
The result of applying a claim to a subject.

A claim about a subject will either be accepted or rejected.

aliases.name: Claim
aliases.comment: Represents a function from a subject to a verdict.

values.name: isEqual
values.comment:
Claim that the subject is equal to the provided value.

The first argument to this function converts a value of some type to a string, which
is used to produce readable messages if the claim is rejected. Instead of providing
this string-generating function each time, I suggest adding a helper function that just uses the
`Debug.toString` function like so:

    equals : a -> Claim a
    equals =
      Spec.Claim.isEqual Debug.toString

Then just use your `equals` function whenever you need to claim that a subject is equal to some value.

values.name: isFalse
values.comment: Claim that the subject is `False`

values.name: isListWhere
values.comment:
Claim that the subject is a list where the following claims are satisfied:

- the subject has the same length as the provided list
- for each item in the subject, that item satisfies the corresponding claim in the provided list.

For example:

    [ 1, 2, 3 ]
      |> Spec.Claim.isListWhere
          [ Spec.Claim.isEqualTo Debug.toString 1
          , Spec.Claim.isEqualTo Debug.toString 27
          , Spec.Claim.isEqualTo Debug.toString 3
          ]

would result in a rejected claim, since 2 is not equal to 27.

values.name: isListWhereItemAt
values.comment:
Claim that the subject is a list such that:

- there is an item at the given index
- that item satisfies the given claim
values.type: Basics.Int -> Spec.Claim.Claim a -> List.List a -> Spec.Claim.Verdict

values.name: isListWithLength
values.comment: Claim that the subject is a list with the given length.

values.name: isNothing
values.comment: Claim that the subject is the `Nothing` case of the `Maybe` type.

values.name: isSomething
values.comment: Claim that the subject is the `Just` case of the `Maybe` type.

values.name: isTrue
values.comment: Claim that the subject is `True`

values.name: satisfying
values.comment:
Combine multiple claims into one.

If any of the claims is rejected, then the combined claim is rejected.

name: Spec.Command
comment:
Functions to work with `Cmd` values in a spec.

@docs send, fake

values.name: fake
values.comment:
Generate a `Cmd` value that will send a `Msg` to the `update`
function provided in the setup of the scenario.

values.name: send
values.comment: A step that sends a command.
values.type: Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg -> Spec.Step.Context model -> Spec.Step.Command msg

name: Spec.Http

unions.name: RequestBody
unions.comment: Represents the body of an HTTP request.

aliases.name: HttpRequest
aliases.comment: Represents an HTTP request made by the program in the course of the scenario.
aliases.type: { url : String.String, headers : Dict.Dict String.String String.String, body : Spec.Http.RequestBody }

values.name: hasHeader
values.comment:
Claim that an HTTP request has a header with the given (key, value) tuple.

For example, if the observed request has an `Authorization` header with the value
`Bearer some-fun-token`, then the following claim:

    Spec.Http.hasHeader ("Authorization", "Bearer some-fun-token")

would be accepted.

values.name: hasJsonBody
values.comment:
Claim that the body of an HTTP request is a string that can be decoded with the
given decoder into a value that satisfies the given claim.

For example, if the body of the observed request was `{"sport":"bowling"}`, then the following claim:

    Spec.Http.hasJsonBody (Json.Decode.field "sport" Json.Decode.string)
      (Spec.Claim.isEqualTo Debug.toString "bowling")

would be accepted.
values.type: Json.Decode.Decoder a -> Spec.Claim.Claim a -> Spec.Http.HttpRequest -> Spec.Claim.Verdict

values.name: hasStringBody
values.comment: Claim that the body of an HTTP request is a string that is equal to the given value.

values.name: observeRequests
values.comment:
Observe HTTP requests that match the given route.

For example:

    Spec.Http.observeRequests (Spec.Http.Route.get "http://fake.com/fake")
      |> Spec.Claim.isList
        [ Spec.Http.hasHeader ("Authorization", "Bearer some-fun-token")
        ]

name: Spec.Http.Route

aliases.name: HttpRoute
aliases.comment: An HTTP route is an HTTP method plus a URL.

values.name: get
values.comment: Define a GET route.

values.name: post
values.comment: Define a POST route.

values.name: route
values.comment:
Define a route with the given method and url.

    Spec.Http.Route.route "PATCH" "http://fake-server.com/fake"

name: Spec.Http.Stub

unions.name: HttpResponseStub
unions.comment: Represents the stubbed response for a particular HTTP request.

values.name: abstain
values.comment:
Abstain from responding to requests to the route defined for the given `HttpResponseStub`.

It can be useful to abstain from responding if you want to describe the behavior of your
program while waiting for a response.

values.name: for
values.comment:
Build a stubbed response for requests made to the given route.

By default, the stubbed response has:

- 200 status code
- no headers
- no body

values.name: serve
values.comment: Set up a fake HTTP server to serve a stubbed response when a matching request is made.
values.type: List.List Spec.Http.Stub.HttpResponseStub -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg

values.name: withBody
values.comment: Supply a body for the stubbed response.

values.name: withHeader
values.comment: Supple a header (key, value) for the stubbed response.

values.name: withNetworkError
values.comment: Set the stubbed response to trigger a network error.

values.name: withStatus
values.comment: Supply a status code for the stubbed response.

values.name: withTimeout
values.comment: Set the stubbed response to trigger a timeout error.

name: Spec.Markup

values.name: hasAttribute
values.comment:
Claim that the HTML element has the given attribute with the given value.

    Spec.Markup.observeElement
      |> Spec.Markup.query << by [ tag "div" ]
      |> Spec.expect (
        Spec.Markup.hasAttribute ("class", "red")
      )

values.name: hasText
values.comment:
Claim that the text belonging to the HTML element contains the given text. 

Note that the text belonging to an observed HTML element includes the text
belonging to all its descendants.

values.name: observe
values.comment:
Observe an HTML element that may not be present in the document.

Use this observer if you want to make a claim about the presence or absence of an HTML element.

    Spec.Markup.observe
      |> Spec.Markup.query << by [ id "some-element" ]
      |> Spec.expect Spec.Claim.isNothing

values.name: observeElement
values.comment:
Observe an HTML element that matches the selector provided to `Spec.Markup.query`.

    Spec.Markup.observeElement
      |> Spec.Markup.query << by [ attribute ("data-attr", "some-value") ]
      |> Spec.expect (Spec.Markup.hasText "something fun")

If the element cannot be found in the document, the claim will be rejected.

values.name: observeElements
values.comment:
Observe all HTML elements that match the selector provided to `Spec.Markup.query`.

    Spec.Markup.observeElements
      |> Spec.Markup.query << by [ attribute ("data-attr", "some-value") ]
      |> Spec.expect (Spec.Claim.isListWithLength 3)

If no elements match the query, then the subject of the claim will be an empty list.

values.name: observeTitle
values.comment:
Observe the title of an HTML document.

Note: It only makes sense to observe the title if your program is constructed with
`Browser.document` or `Browser.application`.

values.name: query
values.comment:
Search for HTML elements.

Use this function in conjunction with `observe`, `observeElement`, or `observeElements` to
observe the HTML document.
values.type: ( Spec.Markup.Selector.Selector Spec.Markup.Selector.Element, Spec.Markup.MarkupObservation a ) -> Spec.Observer.Observer model a

values.name: target
values.comment:
A step that identifies an element to which later steps will be applied.

    Spec.when "the button is clicked twice"
      [ Spec.Markup.target << by [ tag "button" ]
      , Spec.Markup.Event.click
      , Spec.Markup.Event.click
      ]
values.type: ( Spec.Markup.Selector.Selector a, Spec.Step.Context model ) -> Spec.Step.Command msg

name: Spec.Markup.Event

values.name: blur
values.comment:
A step that simulates the targeted HTML element losing focus.

This will trigger a `blur` DOM event on the targeted element.

values.name: click
values.comment:
A step that simulates a click event on the targeted item, either the
document as a whole (using the `Spec.Markup.Selector.document` selector or
some particular HTML element).

This will trigger `mousedown`, `mouseup`, and `click` DOM events on the targeted item.

In several cases, you can use `click` to trigger other kinds of events. Use `click` to
simulate checking or unchecking a checkbox. Use `click` to select a radio button. Use
`click` to submit a form by clicking the associated button.

values.name: doubleClick
values.comment:
A step that simulates a double click on the targeted HTML element.

This will trigger two sets of `mouseup`, `mousedown`, and `click` DOM events and then a `dblclick` DOM event.

values.name: focus
values.comment:
A step that simulates the targeted HTML element receiving focus.

This will trigger a `focus` DOM event on the targeted element.

values.name: hideWindow
values.comment:
A step that simulates hiding the window from view.

This will trigger a `visibilitychange` DOM event on the document object.

values.name: input
values.comment:
A step that simulates text input to the targeted HTML element.

This will set the `value` attribute of the targeted element to the given string
and trigger an `input` DOM event on that element.

To trigger input events on a radio button just target the button and simulate a click on it.

To trigger input events on a select, use `selectOption`.

values.name: mouseDown
values.comment:
A step that simulates pressing the mouse button on the targeted item, either the
document as a whole (using the `Spec.Markup.Selector.document` selector or
some particular HTML element).

This will trigger a `mouseDown` DOM event on the targeted item.

values.name: mouseMoveIn
values.comment:
A step that simulates the mouse moving into the targeted HTML element.

This will trigger `mouseover` and `mouseenter` DOM events on the targeted element.

values.name: mouseMoveOut
values.comment:
A step that simulates the mouse moving out of the targeted HTML element.

This will trigger `mouseout` and `mouseleave` DOM events on the targeted element.

values.name: mouseUp
values.comment:
A step that simulates releasing the mouse button on the targeted item, either the
document as a whole (using the `Spec.Markup.Selector.document` selector or
some particular HTML element).

This will trigger a `mouseup` DOM event on the targeted item.

values.name: resizeWindow
values.comment:
A step that simulates resizing the browser window to the given (width, height).

This will trigger a `resize` DOM event on the window object.
values.type: ( Basics.Int, Basics.Int ) -> Spec.Step.Context model -> Spec.Step.Command msg

values.name: selectOption
values.comment:
A step that simulates selecting an option from the menu generated by a `<select>` element.

This will select the option whose text or label matches the given string, and then trigger `change` and
`input` DOM events on the targeted HTML `<select>` element.

values.name: showWindow
values.comment:
A step that simulates the window returning into view.

This will trigger a `visibilitychange` DOM event on the document object.

values.name: trigger
values.comment:
A step that triggers a custom DOM event on the targeted item.

Provide the name of the DOM event and a JSON value that specifes any properties to add to the event object.

For example, to simulate releasing the `A` key:

    Spec.when "The A key is released"
      [ Spec.Markup.target << by [ id "my-field" ]
      , Json.Encode.object [ ( "keyCode", Encode.int 65 ) ]
          |> Spec.Markup.event.trigger "keyup"
      ]

You may trigger a custom DOM event on the document (by selecting it with `Spec.Markup.Selector.document`)
or some particular HTML element.
values.type: String.String -> Json.Encode.Value -> Spec.Step.Context model -> Spec.Step.Command msg

name: Spec.Markup.Navigation
comment:
Functions for observing navigation changes.

@docs observeLocation, expectReload

values.name: expectReload
values.comment:
Expect that a `Browser.Navigation.reload` or `Browser.Navigation.reloadAndSkipCache`
command was executed.

values.name: observeLocation
values.comment:
Observe the current location of the document.

    Spec.it "has the correct location" (
      observeLocation
        |> Spec.expect (
           Spec.Claim.isEqual Debug.toString
             "http://fake-server.com/something-fun"
        )
    )

This is useful to observe that `Browswer.Navigation.load`,
`Browser.Navigation.pushUrl`, or `Browser.Navigation.replaceUrl` was
executed with the value you expect.

Note that you can use `Spec.Setup.withLocation` to set the base location
of the document at the start of the scenario.

name: Spec.Markup.Selector

unions.name: Characteristic
unions.comment: A selector is built from one or more characteristics.

unions.name: Element
unions.comment: A kind of selector that pertains only to HTML elements.

unions.name: Object
unions.comment: A kind of selector that pertains only to a specific item.

unions.name: Selector
unions.comment: An abstract description of some aspect of an HTML document, usually an HTML element.

values.name: attribute
values.comment:
Generate a characteristic that belongs to elements with the given (name, value) pair.

For example,

    attribute ( "data-some-attr", "some-value" )

applies to elements having an attribute named `data-some-attr` with the value `some-value`.

values.name: attributeName
values.comment:
Generate a characteristic that belongs to elements with the given attribute.

For example, 

    attributeName "data-some-attr"

applies to elements having an attribute names `data-some-attr`.

values.name: by
values.comment: Generate a selector that matches HTML elements with the given characteristics.
values.type: List.List Spec.Markup.Selector.Characteristic -> a -> ( Spec.Markup.Selector.Selector Spec.Markup.Selector.Element, a )

values.name: descendantsOf
values.comment:
Generate a selector that matches HTML elements descending from any HTML elements with the
given characteristics.

For example,

    descendantsOf [ tag "div" ] << by [ tag "span" ]

will match all `<span>` that are inside a `<div>`. You may chain `descendantsOf` selectors
using `<<` but the chain must terminate with a `by` selector.
values.type: List.List Spec.Markup.Selector.Characteristic -> ( Spec.Markup.Selector.Selector Spec.Markup.Selector.Element, a ) -> ( Spec.Markup.Selector.Selector Spec.Markup.Selector.Element, a )

values.name: document
values.comment:
Generate a selector that matches the HTML document.

This is used primarily in conjunction with `Spec.Markup.target` to trigger
document-level events.

For example,

    Spec.when "a document level mouse click is triggered"
      [ Spec.Markup.target << document
      , Spec.Markup.Event.click
      ]

The `document` selector only works with `Spec.Markup.target`, which is to say
that you cannot observe the document to make claims about it.
values.type: Spec.Step.Context model -> ( Spec.Markup.Selector.Selector Spec.Markup.Selector.Object, Spec.Step.Context model )

values.name: id
values.comment:
Generate a characteristic that belongs to elements having
an `id` attribute with the given value.

values.name: tag
values.comment:
Generate a characteristic that belongs to elements having the given tag name.

For example,

    tag "div"

applies to HTML `<div>` elements.

values.name: toString
values.comment:
Convert a Selector to a string.

For those selectors of type `Selector Element`, the resulting string will be in
the style of a CSS selector.

name: Spec.Observer
comment:
An observer evaluates a claim with respect to some part of the world.

This module contains functions for working with observers at a high level.

Check out `Spec.Markup`, `Spec.Http`, `Spec.Port`, and `Spec.Witness` for
more observers.

# Build Observers
@docs Expectation, Observer, observeModel

# Work with Observers
@docs observeResult, mapRejection

aliases.name: Expectation
aliases.comment:
Represents what should be the case about some part of the world.

Expectations are checked at the end of the scenario, after all steps of the
script have been performed.

aliases.name: Observer
aliases.comment:
An `Observer` determines whether to accept or reject a claim with
respect to some particular part of the world. It is a function from a
claim to an `Expectation` that will be evaluted at the end of the scenario.

values.name: mapRejection
values.comment: Create a new `Observer` that will map the `Report` created if the `Claim` is rejected.
values.type: (Spec.Report.Report -> Spec.Report.Report) -> Spec.Observer.Observer model a -> Spec.Observer.Observer model a

values.name: observeModel
values.comment:
Observe some aspect of the model of the program whose behavior is being described in this scenario.

For example, if the program model looks like `{ score: 27 }`, then you could observe the
score like so:

    Spec.it "has the correct score" (
      observeModel .score
        |> Spec.expect 
          (Spec.Claim.isEqual Debug.toString 27)
    )

Check out `Spec.Markup`, `Spec.Http`, `Spec.Port`, and `Spec.Witness` for
observers that evaluate claims with respect to the world outside the program.

values.name: observeResult
values.comment:
Create a new `Observer` that will evaluate a `Claim` with respect to
the successful `Result` observed by the given `Observer`. If the `Result`
fails then the `Claim` will be rejected.
values.type: Spec.Observer.Observer model (Result.Result Spec.Report.Report a) -> Spec.Observer.Observer model a

name: Spec.Port
comment:
Functions for working with ports during a spec.

Suppose your app sends a port command when a button is clicked,
and then displays a message received over a port subscription. You
could write a scenario like so:

    Spec.describe "command ports and subscription ports"
    [ Spec.scenario "send and receive" (
        Spec.given (
          Spec.Setup.withInit (App.init testFlags)
            |> Spec.Setup.withUpdate App.update
            |> Spec.Setup.withView App.view
            |> Spec.Setup.withSubscriptions App.subscriptions
            |> Spec.Port.record "my-command-port"
        )
        |> Spec.when "a message is sent out"
          [ Spec.Markup.target << by [ tag "button" ]
          , Spec.Markup.Event.click
          ]
        |> Spec.when "a response is received"
          [ Spec.Port.send "my-subscription-port" <|
              Json.Encode.object
                [ ("message", Encode.string "Have fun!")
                ]
          ]
        |> Spec.observeThat
          [ Spec.it "sent one message over the port" (
              Spec.Port.observe "my-command-port" someDecoder
                |> Spec.expect (Spec.isListWithLength 1)
            )
          , Spec.it "shows the message received" (
              Spec.Markup.observeElement
                |> Spec.Markup.query << by [ id "message" ]
                |> Spec.expect (Spec.Markup.hasText "Have fun!")
            )
          ]
      )
    ]

# Observe Command Ports
@docs record, observe

# Simulate Subscription Ports
@docs send

values.name: observe
values.comment:
Observe messages sent out via a command port.

Provide the name of the port (the function name) and a decoder that can decode
messages sent out over the port.
values.type: String.String -> Json.Decode.Decoder a -> Spec.Observer.Observer model (List.List a)

values.name: record
values.comment:
Setup the scenario to record messages sent via a command port.

Note: If a port command is sent at any time during the scenario script, you must
use `record` to setup the scenario to record messages on that port.
Otherwise, the scenario may time out.
values.type: String.String -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg

values.name: send
values.comment:
A step that sends a message to a port subscription.

Provide the name of the port and an encoded JSON value that should be sent from the JavaScript side.

For example, if you have a port like so:

    port listenForStuff : (String -> msg) -> Sub msg

Then you could send a message on this port like so:

    Spec.when "a message is sent to the subscription"
    [ Spec.Port.send "listenForStuff" <| Encode.string "Some words"
    ]
values.type: String.String -> Json.Encode.Value -> Spec.Step.Context model -> Spec.Step.Command msg

name: Spec.Report

unions.name: Report
unions.comment:
Represents a collection of notes or facts.

For example, a `Report` can describe why a `Claim` was rejected.

values.name: batch
values.comment: Combine a list of reports into one.

values.name: decoder
values.comment: Decode a `Report` from an appropriate JSON object.

values.name: encoder
values.comment: Encode a `Report` into a JSON object.

values.name: fact
values.comment: Generate a `Report` that is a line of text followed by some details that should be emphasized.

values.name: note
values.comment: Generate a `Report` that is a single line of text.

name: Spec.Runner

aliases.name: Config
aliases.comment:
The spec suite runner must provide a Config, which must be implemented as follows:

Create two ports:

    port elmSpecOut : Message -> Cmd msg
    port elmSpecIn : (Message -> msg) -> Sub msg

And then create a `Config` like so:

    config : Spec.Runner.Config msg
    config =
      { send = elmSpecOut
      , outlet = elmSpecOut
      , listen = elmSpecIn
      }

The `send` and `outlet` attributes must reference the same port, `elmSpecOut`.
aliases.type: { send : Spec.Runner.Message -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd (Spec.Runner.Msg msg), outlet : Spec.Runner.Message -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg, listen : (Spec.Runner.Message -> Spec.Runner.Msg msg) -> Platform.Sub.Sub (Spec.Runner.Msg msg) }

aliases.name: Flags
aliases.comment: Flags that the JavaScript runner will pass to the spec suite program.

aliases.name: Message
aliases.comment: Represents a message to pass between elm-spec and the JavaScript elm-spec runner.

aliases.name: Model
aliases.comment: Used by the spec suite program.

aliases.name: Msg
aliases.comment: Used by the spec suite program.

values.name: browserProgram
values.comment:
Create a spec suite program for describing the behavior of browser-based programs.

Once you've created the `Config` value in your `Runner` module, I suggest adding a function like so:

    program : List (Spec model msg) -> Program Flags (Model model msg) (Msg msg)
    program =
      Spec.Runner.browserProgram config

Then, each of your spec modules can implement their own `main` function:

    main =
      Runner.program
        [ ... some specs ...
        ]

The JavaScript runner will find each spec module and run it as its own program.
values.type: Spec.Runner.Config msg -> List.List (Spec.Spec model msg) -> Platform.Program Spec.Runner.Flags (Spec.Runner.Model model msg) (Spec.Runner.Msg msg)

values.name: program
values.comment:
Create a spec suite program for describing the behavior of headless programs.

Once you've created the `Config` value in your `Runner` module, I suggest adding a function like so:

    program : List (Spec model msg) -> Program Flags (Model model msg) (Msg msg)
    program =
      Spec.Runner.program config

Then, each of your spec modules can implement their own `main` function:

    main =
      Runner.program
        [ ... some specs ...
        ]

The JavaScript runner will find each spec module and run it as its own program.
values.type: Spec.Runner.Config msg -> List.List (Spec.Spec model msg) -> Platform.Program Spec.Runner.Flags (Spec.Runner.Model model msg) (Spec.Runner.Msg msg)

name: Spec.Setup

aliases.name: Setup
aliases.comment: Represents the initial state of the world for a scenario.

values.name: init
values.comment:
Provide an initial program model and command. The command will be executed as
the first step in the scenario script.

You might use `init` in conjunction with the `init` function of the program whose
behavior the scenario describes. In that case you could do something like:

    Spec.given (
      Spec.Setup.init (App.init testFlags)
    )

If you are describing the behavior of a program created with `Browser.application` then
consider using `initForApplication` instead.
values.type: ( model, Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg ) -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg

values.name: initForApplication
values.comment:
Use the init function for a program created with `Browser.application` to set the
initial state for the scenario. You could do something like:

    Spec.given (
      Spec.Setup.initForApplication (App.init testFlags)
    )
values.type: (Url.Url -> Browser.Navigation.Key -> ( model, Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg )) -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg

values.name: initWithModel
values.comment: Provide an initial model for the program whose behavior this scenario describes.

values.name: onUrlChange
values.comment:
If the scenario is describing a program created with `Browser.application`, you can use
this function to supply the program's `onUrlChange` function.

This is most useful if the scenario involves reponding to location changes.
values.type: (Url.Url -> msg) -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg

values.name: onUrlRequest
values.comment:
If the scenario is describing a program created with `Browser.application`, you can use
this function to supply the program's `onUrlRequest` function.

This is most useful if the scenario involves initiating location changes.
values.type: (Browser.UrlRequest -> msg) -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg

values.name: withDocument
values.comment:
Provide the `view` function for the program whose behavior the scenario describes, where
this program is created with `Browser.document` or `Browser.application`.
values.type: (model -> Browser.Document msg) -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg

values.name: withLocation
values.comment:
Set up the scenario to begin with a particular location.

If the program whose behavior is being described was created with `Browser.application` then
this `Url` will be provided to the `init` function. In any case, this location will serve as
the base href, and any location changes will be relative to this location.

By default, the scenario begins with the location `http://elm-spec/`
values.type: Url.Url -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg

values.name: withSubscriptions
values.comment: Provide the `subscriptions` function for the program whose behavior the scenario describes.
values.type: (model -> Platform.Sub.Sub msg) -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg

values.name: withUpdate
values.comment: Provide the `update` function for the program whose behavior the scenario describes.
values.type: (msg -> model -> ( model, Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg )) -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg

values.name: withView
values.comment:
Provide the `view` function for the program whose behavior the scenario describes, where
this program is created with `Browser.sandbox` or `Browser.element`.
values.type: (model -> Html.Html msg) -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg

name: Spec.Step
comment:
A scenario script is a sequence of steps. A step is a function from a `Context`,
which represents the current scenario state, to a `Command`, which describes an action to be
executed before moving to the next step.

See `Spec.Command`, `Spec.Markup`, `Spec.Markup.Event`, `Spec.Port`, and `Spec.Time` for
steps you can use to build a scenario script.

@docs Context, Command

# Using the Context
@docs model

aliases.name: Command
aliases.comment: Represents an action to be performed.

aliases.name: Context
aliases.comment: Represents the current state of the program.

values.name: model
values.comment: Get the current program model from the `Context`.

name: Spec.Time

values.name: nextAnimationFrame
values.comment:
A step that simulates waiting for the next animation frame.

Any subscriptions that depend on animation frame updates will be triggered as expected.

values.name: tick
values.comment:
A step that simulates waiting for some number of milliseconds to pass.

Any subscriptions that depend on the passage of time will be triggered as expected.

values.name: withTime
values.comment:
Set up the scenario at a particular time.

Provide the number of milliseconds since the UNIX epoch.
values.type: Basics.Int -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg

values.name: withTimezoneOffset
values.comment:
Set up the scenario at a particular timezone offset.

Provide the timezone offste in minutes.
values.type: Basics.Int -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg

name: Spec.Witness
comment:
A `Witness` can be used to record information about the arguments passed to
a `Cmd`-generating function.

If you use dependency inversion to decouple part of your program from the
`Cmd`-generating functions it depends upon, you can use a `Witness` to prove that this
part of your program works with the `Cmd`-generating function in the right way.

Let's say I have a `Cmd`-generating function that takes a score (an `Int`) and saves
it to the server via some HTTP request. Let's further suppose I want to describe the behavior
of my user interface, without worrying about the details of how a score is saved. In this
case, I'll 'inject' the `Cmd`-generating function instead of calling it directly from my update
function. This allows me to substitute a fake function during my spec.

Here's the update function:

    update : (Int -> Cmd Msg) -> Msg -> Model -> (Model, Cmd Msg)
    update scoreSaver msg model =
      case msg of
        SaveScore score ->
          ( model, scoreSaver score )
        ...

Now, I can write a spec that uses a witness to record the score passed to the injected
`scoreSaver` function like so:

    Spec.describe "saving the score"
    [ Spec.scenario "successful save" (
        Spec.given (
          Spec.Setup.withInit (App.init testFlags)
            |> Spec.Setup.withView App.view
            |> Witness.forUpdate (\witness ->
              App.update <| \score ->
                Witness.log "saved-score"
                  (Json.Encode.int score) witness
            )
        )
        |> Spec.when "the score is saved"
          [ Spec.Markup.target << by [ id "game-over-button" ]
          , Spec.Markup.Event.click
          ]
        |> it "saves the proper score" (
          Witness.observe "saved-score" (Json.Decode.int)
            |> Spec.expect (Spec.Claim.isList
              [ Spec.Claim.isEqual Debug.toString 28
              ]
            )
        )
      )
    ]

@docs Witness, forUpdate, log, observe

unions.name: Witness
unions.comment: Use a `Witness` to record information from inside a `Cmd`-generating function.

values.name: forUpdate
values.comment:
Set up the scenario with the update function from the program whose behavior is being
described. You'll have access to a `Witness` to use in constructing this function.

See the example above.
values.type: (Spec.Witness.Witness msg -> msg -> model -> ( model, Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg )) -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg -> Spec.Setup.Setup model msg

values.name: log
values.comment:
Create a `Cmd` that logs some information.

Provide the name of this witness and a JSON value with any information to be logged.
values.type: String.String -> Json.Encode.Value -> Spec.Witness.Witness msg -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg

values.name: observe
values.comment:
Observe the logs recorded by a witness.

Provide the name of the witness and a JSON decoder that can decode whatever
value you need to observe.
values.type: String.String -> Json.Decode.Decoder a -> Spec.Observer.Observer model (List.List a)
